documentation:
coordinax

Coordinates in JAX

Coordinax enables calculations with coordinates in [JAX](https://jax.readthedocs.io/en/latest/). Built on [Equinox](https://docs.kidger.site/equinox/) and [Quax](https://github.com/patrick-kidger/quax). ## Installation [![PyPI platforms][pypi-platforms]][pypi-link] [![PyPI version][pypi-version]][pypi-link] ```bash pip install coordinax ``` ## Documentation [![Documentation Status][rtd-badge]][rtd-link] Coming soon. In the meantime, if you've used `astropy.coordinates`, then `coordinax` should be fairly intuitive. ## Quick example ```python import jax.numpy as jnp import unxt as u import coordinax as cx q = cx.CartesianPos3D( x=u.Quantity(jnp.arange(0, 10.0), "km"), y=u.Quantity(jnp.arange(5, 15.0), "km"), z=u.Quantity(jnp.arange(10, 20.0), "km"), ) print(q) # q2 = cx.represent_as(q, cx.SphericalPos) print(q2) # p = cx.CartesianVel3D( d_x=u.Quantity(jnp.arange(0, 10.0), "m/s"), d_y=u.Quantity(jnp.arange(5, 15.0), "m/s"), d_z=u.Quantity(jnp.arange(10, 20.0), "m/s"), ) print(p) # p2 = cx.represent_as(p, cx.SphericalVel, q) print(p2) # ``` ## Citation [![DOI][zenodo-badge]][zenodo-link] If you found this library to be useful in academic work, then please cite. ## Development [![Actions Status][actions-badge]][actions-link] We welcome contributions! [actions-badge]: https://github.com/GalacticDynamics/coordinax/workflows/CI/badge.svg [actions-link]: https://github.com/GalacticDynamics/coordinax/actions [pypi-link]: https://pypi.org/project/coordinax/ [pypi-platforms]: https://img.shields.io/pypi/pyversions/coordinax [pypi-version]: https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/coordinax [rtd-badge]: https://readthedocs.org/projects/coordinax/badge/?version=latest [rtd-link]: https://coordinax.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest [zenodo-badge]: https://zenodo.org/badge/755708966.svg [zenodo-link]: https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.10850557

functions.name: coordinax._interop.coordinax_interop_astropy.distance.convert_astropy_quantity_to_unxt_distance
documentation:
Convert a `astropy.units.Quantity` to a `coordinax.Distance`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from astropy.units import Quantity as AstropyQuantity
>>> from plum import convert
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> convert(AstropyQuantity(1.0, "cm"), cx.Distance)
Distance(Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='cm')
```
Distance

documentation:

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from plum import convert
>>> from coordinax.distance import Parallax
```

```pycon
>>> convert(AstropyQuantity(1.0, "radian"), Parallax)
Parallax(Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='rad')
```
Parallax

variables.name: coordinax.setup_package.RUNTIME_TYPECHECKER
documentation:
Runtime type checking variable "COORDINAX_ENABLE_RUNTIME_TYPECHECKING".

Set to "None" to disable runtime typechecking (default). Set to
"beartype.beartype" to enable runtime typechecking.

See https://docs.kidger.site/jaxtyping/api/runtime-type-checking for more
information on options.

classes.name: coordinax._coordinax_space_frames.galactocentric.Galactocentric
documentation:
Reference frame centered at the Galactic center.

Based on the Astropy implementation of the Galactocentric frame.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> frame = cx.frames.Galactocentric()
>>> frame
Galactocentric(
  galcen=LonLatSphericalPos( ... ),
  roll=Quantity[...](value=weak_i32[], unit=Unit("deg")),
  z_sun=Quantity[...](value=weak_f32[], unit=Unit("pc")),
  galcen_v_sun=CartesianVel3D( ... )
)
```

functions.name: coordinax._coordinax_space_frames._interop.interop_astropy.frames.from_
documentation:
Construct from a `astropy.coordinates.Galactocentric`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import astropy.coordinates as apyc
>>> import coordinax.frames as cxf
```

```pycon
>>> apy_gcf = apyc.Galactocentric()
>>> apy_gcf
<Galactocentric Frame (galcen_coord=<ICRS Coordinate: (ra, dec) in deg
(266.4051, -28.936175)>, galcen_distance=8.122 kpc, galcen_v_sun=(12.9, 245.6, 7.78) km / s, z_sun=20.8 pc, roll=0.0 deg)>
```

```pycon
>>> gcf = cxf.Galactocentric.from_(apy_gcf)
>>> gcf
Galactocentric(
    galcen=LonLatSphericalPos( ... ),
    roll=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("deg")),
    z_sun=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("pc")),
    galcen_v_sun=CartesianVel3D( ... )
)
```

Checking equality

```pycon
>>> (gcf.galcen.lon.to_value("deg") == apy_gcf.galcen_coord.ra.to_value("deg")
...  and gcf.galcen.lat.to_value("deg") == apy_gcf.galcen_coord.dec.to_value("deg")
...  and gcf.galcen.distance.to_value("kpc") == apy_gcf.galcen_distance.to_value("kpc") )
Array(True, dtype=bool)
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.utils.classproperty

params.name: Doc("function to be used for getting a class-level attribute value.")

documentation:
Create a class-level property.

This is most commonly used as a decorator for a function that returns a
class-level attribute value.

# Parameters

func

: The function to be used as the getter.

# Returns

ClassPropertyDescriptor\[EnclosingT, GetterRetT\]

: The class-level property.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from coordinax._src.utils import classproperty
>>> class MyConstants:
...     @classproperty
...     def pau(cls) -> float:
...         return 4.71
>>> MyConstants.pau
4.71
```

classes.name: coordinax._src.utils.ClassPropertyDescriptor

methods.name: coordinax._src.utils.ClassPropertyDescriptor.__get__
documentation:
Get the class-level attribute value.

# Parameters

obj

: The object this descriptor is being accessed from. If `None` then
  this is being accessed from the class, not an instance.

klass

: The class this descriptor is being accessed from. If `None` then
  this is being accessed from an instance, not the class.

# Returns

GetterRetT

: The class-level attribute value.

# Raises

TypeError

: If the descriptor is accessed from neither an instance nor a class.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from coordinax._src.utils import classproperty
>>> class MyConstants:
...     @classproperty
...     def pau(cls) -> float:
...         return 4.71
>>> MyConstants.pau
4.71
```

Class properties can only be accessed from the class itself (or its instances):

```pycon
>>> clsprop = ClassPropertyDescriptor(staticmethod(lambda: 10.0))
>>> try: clsprop.__get__(None, None)
... except TypeError as e: print(e)
Descriptor must be accessed from an instance or class.
```

From the class:

```pycon
>>> clsprop.__get__(None, MyConstants)
10.0
```

From an instance:

```pycon
>>> clsprop.__get__(MyConstants(), None)
10.0
```

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.utils.ClassPropertyDescriptor.fget
documentation: function to be used for getting a class-level attribute value.
type: ignore[type-arg]

Descriptor for class properties.

# Parameters

fget

: The class/staticmethod wrapped function to be used as the getter for the
  class-level property.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from coordinax._src.utils import classproperty
>>> class MyConstants:
...     @classproperty
...     def pau(cls) -> float:
...         return 4.71
>>> MyConstants.pau
4.71
```

functions.name: coordinax._interop.coordinax_interop_astropy.constructors.from_
documentation:
Construct a vector from a mapping.

# Parameters

cls

: The vector class.

obj

: The mapping of components.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import jax.numpy as jnp
>>> from astropy.units import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> xs = {"x": Quantity(1, "m"), "y": Quantity(2, "m"), "z": Quantity(3, "m")}
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(xs)
>>> vec
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

```pycon
>>> xs = {"x": Quantity([1, 2], "m"), "y": Quantity([3, 4], "m"),
...       "z": Quantity([5, 6], "m")}
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(xs)
>>> vec
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

functions.name: coordinax._interop.coordinax_interop_astropy.constructors.from_
documentation:


```pycon
>>> cart = CartesianRepresentation(1, 2, 3, unit="kpc")

>>> vec.x
```

functions.name: coordinax._interop.coordinax_interop_astropy.constructors.from_
documentation:
Construct a vector from an Astropy Quantity array.

The array is expected to have the components as the last dimension.

# Parameters

cls

: The vector class.

obj

: The array of components.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import jax.numpy as jnp
>>> from astropy.units import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> xs = Quantity([1, 2, 3], "meter")
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(xs)
>>> vec
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

```pycon
>>> xs = Quantity(jnp.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]), "meter")
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(xs)
>>> vec
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m"))
)
>>> vec.x
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 4.], dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_(Quantity([1, 2, 3], "m/s"))
>>> vec
CartesianVel3D(
  d_x=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
  d_y=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
  d_z=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s") )
)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_(Quantity([1, 2, 3], "m/s2"))
>>> vec
CartesianAcc3D(
  d2_x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s2")),
  d2_y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s2")),
  d2_z=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s2"))
)
```

```pycon
>>> xs = Quantity([0, 1, 2, 3], "meter")  # [ct, x, y, z]
>>> vec = cx.FourVector.from_(xs)
>>> vec
FourVector(
    t=Quantity[PhysicalType('time')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m s / km")),
    q=CartesianPos3D( ... )
)
```

```pycon
>>> xs = Quantity(jnp.array([[0, 1, 2, 3], [10, 4, 5, 6]]), "meter")
>>> vec = cx.FourVector.from_(xs)
>>> vec
FourVector(
    t=Quantity[PhysicalType('time')](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m s / km")),
    q=CartesianPos3D( ... )
)
>>> vec.x
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 4.], dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.operators.sequential.simplify_op
documentation:
Simplify a sequence of Operators.

This simplifies the sequence of operators by removing any that reduce to
the Identity operator.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax.operators as co
```

```pycon
>>> shift = co.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator(u.Quantity([1, 2, 3], "kpc"))
>>> boost = co.GalileanBoostOperator(u.Quantity([10, 20, 30], "km/s"))
```

```pycon
>>> seq = shift | co.IdentityOperator() | boost
>>> seq
OperatorSequence(
  operators=( GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator( ... ),
              IdentityOperator(),
              GalileanBoostOperator( ... ) )
)
```

```pycon
>>> co.simplify_op(seq3)
OperatorSequence(
  operators=( GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator( ... ),
              GalileanBoostOperator( ... ) )
)
```
Functions

classes.name: coordinax._src.operators.sequential.OperatorSequence

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.sequential.OperatorSequence.__or__
documentation: Compose with another operator.

Sequence of operations.

This is the composite operator that represents a sequence of operations to
be applied in order.

# Parameters

operators

: The sequence of operators to apply.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax.operators as co
```

```pycon
>>> shift = co.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator(u.Quantity([1, 2, 3], "kpc"))
>>> boost = co.GalileanBoostOperator(u.Quantity([10, 20, 30], "km/s"))
>>> seq = co.OperatorSequence((shift, boost))
>>> seq
OperatorSequence(
  operators=( GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator( ... ),
              GalileanBoostOperator( ... ) )
)
```

A sequence of operators can also be constructed by `|`:

```pycon
>>> seq2 = shift | boost
>>> seq2
OperatorSequence(
  operators=( GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator( ... ),
              GalileanBoostOperator( ... ) )
)
```

The sequence of operators can be simplified. For this example, we
add an identity operator to the sequence:

```pycon
>>> seq3 = seq2 | co.IdentityOperator()
>>> seq3
OperatorSequence(
  operators=( GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator( ... ),
              GalileanBoostOperator( ... ),
              IdentityOperator() )
)
```

```pycon
>>> co.simplify_op(seq3)
OperatorSequence(
  operators=( GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator( ... ),
              GalileanBoostOperator( ... ) )
)
```

methods.name: coordinax._coordinax_space_frames.frame_transforms._ICRS2GCFOperator.__call__
documentation: Transform q and p from ICRS Cartesian -> GCF Cartesian.

Transform from ICRS to Galactocentric frame.

:::{warning}
This operator is temporary and will be replaced as an operator Sequence
of the individual transformations.
:::

# Examples

For this example we compare against the Astropy implementation of the
frame transformation:

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> import astropy.coordinates as apyc
```

```pycon
>>> vega = apyc.SkyCoord(
...     ra=279.23473479 * u.unit("deg"), dec=38.78368896 * u.unit("deg"),
...     distance=25 * u.unit("pc"),
...     pm_ra_cosdec=200 * u.unit("mas / yr"), pm_dec=-286 * u.unit("mas / yr"),
...     radial_velocity=-13.9 * u.unit("km / s"))
>>> print(vega)
<SkyCoord (ICRS): (ra, dec, distance) in (deg, deg, pc)
    (279.23473479, 38.78368896, 25.)
 (pm_ra_cosdec, pm_dec, radial_velocity) in (mas / yr, mas / yr, km / s)
    (200., -286., -13.9)>
```

```pycon
>>> apy_gcf = apyc.Galactocentric()
>>> apy_gcf
<Galactocentric Frame (galcen_coord=<ICRS Coordinate: (ra, dec) in deg
    (266.4051, -28.936175)>, galcen_distance=8.122 kpc, galcen_v_sun=(12.9, 245.6, 7.78) km / s, z_sun=20.8 pc, roll=0.0 deg)>
```

```pycon
>>> vega.transform_to(apy_gcf)
<SkyCoord (Galactocentric: ...): (x, y, z) in pc
    (-8112.89970167, 21.79911216, 29.01384942)
 (v_x, v_y, v_z) in km / s
    (34.06711868, 234.61647066, -28.75976702)>
```

Now we can use the `_ICRS2GCFOperator` to perform the same transformation:

```pycon
>>> vega_q = cx.LonLatSphericalPos.from_(vega.icrs.data)
>>> vega_p = cx.LonCosLatSphericalVel.from_(vega.icrs.data.differentials["s"])
```

```pycon
>>> icrs_frame = cx.frames.ICRS()
>>> gcf_frame = cx.frames.Galactocentric.from_(apy_gcf)
```

```pycon
>>> frame_op = cx.frames.frame_transform_op(icrs_frame, gcf_frame)
>>> vega_gcf_q, vega_gcf_p = frame_op(vega_q, vega_p)
>>> print(vega_gcf_q)
<CartesianPos3D (x[pc], y[pc], z[pc])
    [-8112.899    21.799    29.014]>
>>> print(vega_gcf_p.uconvert({"speed": "km/s"}))
<CartesianVel3D (d_x[km / s], d_y[km / s], d_z[km / s])
    [ 34.067 234.616 -28.76 ]>
```

It matches!

documentation: Transform q and p from GCF Cartesian -> ICRS Cartesian.

Transform from Galactocentric to ICRS frame.

:::{warning}
This operator is temporary and will be replaced as an operator Sequence
of the individual transformations.
:::

# Examples

For this example we compare against the Astropy implementation of the
frame transformation:

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> import astropy.coordinates as apyc
```

```pycon
>>> apy_gcf = apyc.Galactocentric()
>>> apy_gcf
<Galactocentric Frame (galcen_coord=<ICRS Coordinate: (ra, dec) in deg
    (266.4051, -28.936175)>, galcen_distance=8.122 kpc, galcen_v_sun=(12.9, 245.6, 7.78) km / s, z_sun=20.8 pc, roll=0.0 deg)>
```

```pycon
>>> vega = apyc.SkyCoord(
...     ra=279.23473479 * u.unit("deg"), dec=38.78368896 * u.unit("deg"),
...     distance=25 * u.unit("pc"),
...     pm_ra_cosdec=200 * u.unit("mas / yr"), pm_dec=-286 * u.unit("mas / yr"),
...     radial_velocity=-13.9 * u.unit("km / s")
... ).transform_to(apy_gcf)
>>> print(vega)
<SkyCoord (Galactocentric: ...): (x, y, z) in pc
    (-8112.89970167, 21.79911216, 29.01384942)
 (v_x, v_y, v_z) in km / s
    (34.06711868, 234.61647066, -28.75976702)>
```

```pycon
>>> vega.transform_to(apyc.ICRS())
<SkyCoord (ICRS): (ra, dec, distance) in (deg, deg, pc)
    (279.23473479, 38.78368896, 25.)
 (pm_ra_cosdec, pm_dec, radial_velocity) in (mas / yr, mas / yr, km / s)
    (200., -286., -13.9)>
```

Now we can use the `_GCF2ICRSOperator` to perform the same transformation:

```pycon
>>> vega_q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(vega.galactocentric.data)
>>> vega_p = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_(vega.galactocentric.data.differentials["s"])
```

```pycon
>>> icrs_frame = cx.frames.ICRS()
>>> gcf_frame = cx.frames.Galactocentric.from_(apy_gcf)
```

```pycon
>>> frame_op = cx.frames.frame_transform_op(gcf_frame, icrs_frame)
>>> vega_icrs_q, vega_icrs_p = frame_op(vega_q, vega_p)
```

```pycon
>>> vega_icrs_q = vega_icrs_q.represent_as(cx.LonLatSphericalPos)
>>> print(vega_icrs_q.uconvert({"angle": "deg", "length": "pc"}))
<LonLatSphericalPos (lon[deg], lat[deg], distance[pc])
    [279.235  38.785  25.   ]>
```

```pycon
>>> vega_icrs_p = vega_icrs_p.represent_as(cx.LonCosLatSphericalVel, vega_icrs_q)
>>> print(vega_icrs_p.uconvert({"angular speed": "mas / yr", "speed": "km/s"}))
<LonCosLatSphericalVel (d_lon_coslat[mas / yr], d_lat[mas / yr], d_distance[km / s])
    [ 200.002 -286.002  -13.9  ]>
```

It matches!

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.identity.IdentityOperator.__call__
documentation:
Apply the Identity operation.

This is the identity operation, which does nothing to the input.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> op = cx.operators.IdentityOperator()
```

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> op(q) is q
True
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> op(vec) is vec
True
```
Dispatched call signatures
More call signatures are registered in the `coordinax._d<X>.operate` modules.

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.identity.IdentityOperator.__call__
documentation:
Apply the Identity operation.

This is the identity operation, which does nothing to the input.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> op = cx.operators.IdentityOperator()
```

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> t = u.Quantity(10, "Gyr")
```

```pycon
>>> op(q, t) == (q, t)
True
```

```pycon
>>> op(vec, t) == (vec, t)
True
```

Identity operation.

This is the identity operation, which does nothing to the input.

# Examples

We will work through many of the registered call signatures for the
`IdentityOperator` class. Note that more call signatures may be registered.

First, we make an instance of the operator:

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> op = cx.operators.IdentityOperator()
>>> op
IdentityOperator()
```

And the common objects we will use:

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(q)
```

The first call signature is for the case where the input is a vector:

```pycon
>>> op(vec) is vec
True
```

The second call signature is for a Quantity:

```pycon
>>> op(q) is q
True
```

Actually, the `Identity` operator works for any vector or quantity:

- 1D:

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1], "kpc")
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos1D.from_(q)
>>> op(vec) is vec and op(q) is q
True
```

- 2D:

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1, 2], "kpc")
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_(q)
>>> op(vec) is vec and op(q) is q
True
```

- 3D (not using a `~coordinax.CartesianPos3D` instance):

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(q).represent_as(cx.SphericalPos)
>>> op(vec) is vec and op(q) is q
True
```

- 4D:

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1, 2, 3, 4], "kpc")  # 0th elt is ct
>>> vec4 = cx.FourVector.from_(q)
>>> op(vec4) is vec4 and op(q) is q
True
```

Lastly, many operators are time dependent and support a time argument. The\`
`Identity` operator will also pass through the time argument:

```pycon
>>> t = u.Quantity(0, "Gyr")
>>> op(vec, t) == (vec, t)
True
```

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> op(q, t) == (q, t)
True
```

documentation: Apply the operator to the coordinates `x` at a time `t`.

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.base.AbstractOperator.is_inertial
documentation: Whether the operation maintains an inertial reference frame.

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.base.AbstractOperator.inverse
documentation: The inverse of the operator.

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.base.AbstractOperator.__or__
documentation: Compose with another operator.
Sequence

Abstract base class for operators on coordinates and potentials.

An operator is an object that defines a transformation on coordinates. It
can be applied to a set of coordinates to produce a new set of coordinates.
Operators can be composed together to form a sequence of transformations.

When defining a new operator, it should be able to work on
`coordinax.AbstractVector` objects. If it is a spatial operator, it should
also be able to work on (`coordinax.AbstractPos`, `unxt.Quantity['time']`)
pairs (and then also `coordinax.FourVector` objects). If the vector can be
created from a `unxt.Quantity` object, then the operator should also be able
to work on `unxt.Quantity` object.

```pycon
>>> import astropy.units as apyu
```

```pycon
...                       theta=apyu.Quantity(2, unit="deg"),
...                       phi=apyu.Quantity(3, unit="deg"))

<Quantity [0.03485167, 0.0018265 , 0.99939084] kpc>
```

```pycon
...                         phi=apyu.Quantity(2, unit="deg"),
...                         z=apyu.Quantity(3, unit="pc"))

<Quantity [0.99939084, 0.0348995 , 0.003     ] kpc>
```

ruff: noqa: ERA001

functions.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.boost.simplify_op
documentation:
Simplify a boost operator.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

An operator with real effect cannot be simplified:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanBoostOperator.from_([1, 0, 0], "m/s")
>>> cxo.simplify_op(op)
GalileanBoostOperator(
  velocity=CartesianVel3D( ... )
)
```

An operator with no effect can be simplified:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanBoostOperator.from_([0, 0, 0], "m/s")
>>> cxo.simplify_op(op)
IdentityOperator()
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.boost.GalileanBoostOperator.__call__
documentation:
Apply the boost to the coordinates.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> op = cx.operators.GalileanBoostOperator.from_([1, 2, 3], "m/s")
```

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([0, 0, 0], "m")
>>> t = u.Quantity(1, "s")
>>> newq, newt = op(q, t)
>>> newt
Quantity['time'](Array(1, dtype=int32, ...), unit='s')
>>> print(newq)
<CartesianPos3D (x[m], y[m], z[m])
    [1. 2. 3.]>
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.boost.GalileanBoostOperator.__call__
documentation: Apply the boost to the coordinates.

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.boost.GalileanBoostOperator.velocity
documentation:
The boost velocity.

This parameters uses {meth}`vector.CartesianVel3D.from_` to enable a variety
of more convenient input types. See {class}`vector.CartesianVel3D` for
details.

Operator for Galilean boosts.

The coordinate transform is given by:

$$
(t,\mathbf{x}) \mapsto (t, \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{v} t)
$$

where $\mathbf{v}$ is the boost velocity.

# Parameters

velocity

: The boost velocity. This parameters uses
  {meth}`vector.CartesianVel3D.from_` to enable a variety of more
  convenient input types. See {class}`vector.CartesianVel3D` for details.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanBoostOperator.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "m/s")
>>> op
GalileanBoostOperator( velocity=CartesianVel3D( ... ) )
```

Note that the velocity is a {class}`vector.CartesianVel3D`, which was
constructed from an array, using {meth}`vector.CartesianVel3D.from_`.

modules.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.composite
documentation: Galilean coordinate transformations.
ruff: noqa: ERA001

functions.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.composite.simplify_op
documentation:
Simplify a Galilean operator.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import jax.numpy as jnp
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

This Galilean operator cannot be simplified:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanOperator(
...     translation=u.Quantity([0., 2., 3., 4.], "kpc"),
...     velocity=u.Quantity([1., 2., 3.], "km/s"),
...     rotation=jnp.eye(3).at[0, 2].set(1),
... )
>>> op
GalileanOperator(
  rotation=GalileanRotationOperator(rotation=f32[3,3]),
  translation=GalileanTranslationOperator(
    translation=FourVector(
      t=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("kpc s / km")),
      q=CartesianPos3D( ... ) )
  ),
  velocity=GalileanBoostOperator( velocity=CartesianVel3D( ... ) )
)
```

```pycon
>>> cxo.simplify_op(op) is op
True
```

This Galilean operator can be simplified in all its components except the
translation:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanOperator(translation=u.Quantity([0., 2., 3., 4.], "kpc"))
>>> cxo.simplify_op(op)
OperatorSequence(
  operators=( GalileanTranslationOperator( translation=FourVector( ... ) ), )
)
```

classes.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.composite.GalileanOperator

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.composite.GalileanOperator.operators
documentation: Rotation -> translation -> boost.

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.composite.GalileanOperator.rotation
documentation: The in-frame spatial rotation.

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.composite.GalileanOperator.translation
documentation:
The temporal + spatial translation.

The translation vector \[T, Q\].  This parameters accetps either a
{class}`coordinax.operators.GalileanTranslationOperator` instance or
any input that can be used to construct a {meth}`vector.FourVector`, using
{meth}`vector.FourVector.from_`. See {class}`vector.FourVector` for
details.

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.composite.GalileanOperator.velocity
documentation:
The boost to the frame.

This parameters accepts either a
{class}`coordinax.operators.GalileanBoostOperator` instance or any input
that can be used to construct a {class}`vector.CartesianVel3D`, using
{meth}`vector.CartesianVel3D.from_`. See {class}`vector.CartesianVel3D` for
details.

Operator for general Galilean transformations.

In the transformed frame the coordinates are given by:

$$
(t,\mathbf{x}) \mapsto (t+s, R \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{v} t + \mathbf{a})
$$

where $R$ is the rotation matrix, $\mathbf{v}$ is the boost
velocity, $\mathbf{a}$ is the spatial translationl, and $s$ is
the time translation. This is equivalent to a sequential operation of 1. a
rotation, 2. a translation, 3. a boost.

# Parameters

translation

: The spatial translation of the frame. See
  {class}`coordinax.operators.GalileanTranslationOperator` for alternative
  inputs to construct this parameter.

velocity

: The boost to the frame. See
  {class}`coordinax.operators.GalileanBoostOperator` for alternative
  inputs to construct this parameter.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanOperator(
...     translation=u.Quantity([0., 2., 3., 4.], "kpc"),
...     velocity=u.Quantity([1., 2., 3.], "km/s"))
>>> op
GalileanOperator(
  rotation=GalileanRotationOperator(rotation=f32[3,3]),
  translation=GalileanTranslationOperator(
    translation=FourVector(
      t=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("kpc s / km")),
      q=CartesianPos3D( ... ) )
  ),
  velocity=GalileanBoostOperator( velocity=CartesianVel3D( ... ) )
)
```

Note that the translation is a
{class}`coordinax.operators.GalileanTranslationOperator` with a
{class}`vector.FourVector` translation, and the velocity is a
{class}`coordinax.operators.GalileanBoostOperator` with a
{class}`vector.CartesianVel3D` velocity. We can also construct them
directly, which allows for other vector types.

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanOperator(
...     translation=cxo.GalileanTranslationOperator(
...         cx.FourVector(t=u.Quantity(2.5, "Gyr"),
...                       q=cx.SphericalPos(r=u.Quantity(1, "kpc"),
...                                         theta=u.Quantity(90, "deg"),
...                                         phi=u.Quantity(0, "rad") ) ) ),
...     velocity=cxo.GalileanBoostOperator(
...         cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "km/s") )
... )
>>> op
GalileanOperator(
  rotation=GalileanRotationOperator(rotation=f32[3,3]),
  translation=GalileanTranslationOperator(
    translation=FourVector(
      t=Quantity[...)](value=f32[], unit=Unit("Gyr")),
      q=SphericalPos( ... )
    )
  ),
  velocity=GalileanBoostOperator( velocity=CartesianVel3D( ... ) )
)
```

Galilean operators can be applied to {class}`vector.FourVector`:

```pycon
>>> w = cx.FourVector.from_([0, 0, 0, 0], "kpc")
>>> new = op(w)
>>> new
FourVector(
  t=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("kpc s / km")),
  q=CartesianPos3D( ... )
)
>>> new.t.ustrip("Gyr").round(2)
Array(2.5, dtype=float32)
>>> new.q.x
Quantity['length'](Array(3.5567803, dtype=float32), unit='kpc')
```

Also the Galilean operators can also be applied to
{class}`vector.AbstractPos3D` and {class}`unxt.Quantity`:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([0, 0, 0], "kpc")
>>> t = u.Quantity(0, "Gyr")
>>> newq, newt = op(q, t)
>>> newq.x
Quantity['length'](Array(3.5567803, dtype=float32), unit='kpc')
>>> newt
Quantity['time'](Array(2.5, dtype=float32), unit='Gyr')
```

A null reference frame.

This is a reference frame that cannot be transformed to or from.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax.frames as cxf
```

```pycon
>>> null = cxf.NoFrame()
>>> icrs = cxf.ICRS()
```

```pycon
>>> try:
...     cxf.frame_transform_op(null, icrs)
... except cxf.FrameTransformError as e:
...     print(e)
Cannot transform from the null frame.
```

```pycon
>>> try:
...     cxf.frame_transform_op(icrs, null)
... except cxf.FrameTransformError as e:
...     print(e)
Cannot transform to the null frame.
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.rotation.simplify_op
documentation:
Simplify the Galilean rotation operator.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import quaxed.numpy as jnp
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

An operator with a non-identity rotation matrix is not simplified:

```pycon
>>> theta = u.Quantity(45, "deg")
>>> Rz = jnp.asarray([[jnp.cos(theta), -jnp.sin(theta), 0],
...                  [jnp.sin(theta), jnp.cos(theta),  0],
...                  [0,             0,              1]])
>>> op = cxo.GalileanRotationOperator(Rz)
>>> cxo.simplify_op(op)
GalileanRotationOperator(rotation=f32[3,3])
```

An operator with an identity rotation matrix is simplified:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanRotationOperator(jnp.eye(3))
>>> cxo.simplify_op(op)
IdentityOperator()
```

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.rotation.GalileanRotationOperator.rotation
documentation: The rotation vector.

Operator for Galilean rotations.

The coordinate transform is given by:

$$
(t,\mathbf{x}) \mapsto (t, R \mathbf{x})
$$

where $R$ is the rotation matrix.  Note this is NOT time dependent.

# Parameters

rotation

: The rotation matrix.

# Raises

ValueError

: If the rotation matrix is not orthogonal.

# Notes

The Galilean rotation is not a time-dependent transformation.  This is part
of the inhomogeneous Galilean group, which is the group of transformations
that leave the space-time interval invariant.

# Examples

We start with the required imports:

```pycon
>>> import jax.numpy as jnp
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

We can then create a rotation operator:

```pycon
>>> theta = jnp.pi / 4  # 45 degrees
>>> Rz = jnp.asarray([[jnp.cos(theta), -jnp.sin(theta), 0],
...                   [jnp.sin(theta), jnp.cos(theta),  0],
...                   [0,              0,               1]])
>>> op = cx.operators.GalileanRotationOperator(Rz)
>>> op
GalileanRotationOperator(rotation=f32[3,3])
```

Translation operators can be applied to a Quantity\[float, (N, 3), "...\]:

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([1, 0, 0], "m")
>>> t = u.Quantity(1, "s")
>>> newq, newt = op(q, t)
>>> newq
Quantity['length'](Array([0.70710677, 0.70710677, 0. ], dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

The time is not affected by the rotation.

```pycon
>>> newt
Quantity['time'](Array(1, dtype=int32, ...), unit='s')
```

This also works for a batch of vectors:

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([[1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0]], "m")
>>> t = u.Quantity(0, "s")
```

```pycon
>>> newq, newt = op(q, t)
>>> newq
Quantity['length'](Array([[ 0.70710677,  0.70710677,  0.        ],
                          [-0.70710677,  0.70710677,  0.        ]], dtype=float32),
                   unit='m')
```

Translation operators can be applied to {class}`vector.AbstractPos3D`:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(q)  # from the previous example
>>> newq, newt = op(q, t)
>>> newq.x
Quantity['length'](Array([ 0.70710677, -0.70710677], dtype=float32), unit='m')
>>> newq.norm().value.round(2)
Array([1., 1.], dtype=float32)
```

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.translation.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator.translation
documentation:
The spatial translation.

This parameters accepts either a {class}`vector.AbstractVector` instance or
uses a Cartesian vector [from] to enable a variety of more convenient
input types to create a Cartesian vector. See
{class}`vector.CartesianPos3D.from_` for an example when doing a 3D
translation.

Operator for Galilean spatial translations.

The coordinate transform is given by:

$$
(t,\mathbf{x}) \mapsto (t+s, \mathbf{x} + \mathbf {a})
$$

# Parameters

translation

: The spatial translation vector. This parameters accepts either a
  {class}`vector.AbstractPos3D` instance or uses
  {meth}`vector.CartesianPos3D.from_` to enable a variety of more
  convenient input types to create a Cartesian vector. See
  {class}`vector.CartesianPos3D` for details.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import quaxed.numpy as jnp
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

We can then create a spatial translation operator:

```pycon
>>> shift = u.Quantity([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "kpc")
>>> op = cxo.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator(shift)
>>> op
GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator( translation=CartesianPos3D( ... ) )
```

Note that the translation is a {class}`vector.CartesianPos3D`, which was
constructed from a 1D array, using {meth}`vector.CartesianPos3D.from_`. We
can also construct it directly, which allows for other vector types.

```pycon
>>> shift = cx.SphericalPos(r=u.Quantity(1.0, "kpc"),
...                         theta=u.Quantity(jnp.pi/2, "rad"),
...                         phi=u.Quantity(0, "rad"))
>>> op = cxo.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator(shift)
>>> op
GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator( translation=SphericalPos( ... ) )
```

Translation operators can be applied to {class}`vector.AbstractPos`:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([0, 0, 0], "kpc")
>>> op(q)
CartesianPos3D( ... )
```

And to {class}`~unxt.Quantity`:

```pycon
>>> q = u.Quantity([0, 0, 0], "kpc")
>>> op(q).value.round(2)
Array([ 1.,  0., -0.], dtype=float32)
```

{class}`coordinax.operators.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator` can be used
for other dimensional vectors as well:

- 1D:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator.from_([1], "kpc")
>>> q = u.Quantity([0], "kpc")
>>> op(q)
Quantity['length'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='kpc')
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos1D.from_(q).represent_as(cx.RadialPos)
>>> op(vec)
RadialPos(r=Distance(value=f32[], unit=Unit("kpc")))
```

- 2D:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator.from_([1, 2], "kpc")
>>> q = u.Quantity([0, 0], "kpc")
>>> op(q)
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 2.], dtype=float32), unit='kpc')
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_(q).represent_as(cx.PolarPos)
>>> op(vec)
PolarPos( r=Distance(value=f32[], unit=Unit("kpc")),
          phi=Angle(value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad")) )
```

- 3D:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanSpatialTranslationOperator.from_([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> q = u.Quantity([0, 0, 0], "kpc")
>>> op(q)
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 2., 3.], dtype=float32), unit='kpc')
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(q).represent_as(cx.SphericalPos)
>>> op(vec)
SphericalPos( r=Distance(value=f32[], unit=Unit("kpc")),
              theta=Angle(value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad")),
              phi=Angle(value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad")) )
```

Many operators are time dependent and require a time argument. This operator
is time independent and will pass through the time argument:

```pycon
>>> t = u.Quantity(0, "Gyr")
>>> op(q, t)[1] is t
True
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.translation.GalileanTranslationOperator.__call__
documentation:
Apply the translation to the coordinates.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

Explicitly construct the translation operator:

```pycon
>>> qshift = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 1, 1], "kpc")
>>> shift = FourVector(u.Quantity(1, "Gyr"), qshift)
>>> op = cxo.GalileanTranslationOperator(shift)
```

Construct a vector to translate, using the convenience [from] (the
0th component is $c * t$, the rest are spatial components):

```pycon
>>> w = cx.FourVector.from_([0, 1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> w.t
Quantity['time'](Array(0., dtype=float32), unit='kpc s / km')
```

Apply the translation operator:

```pycon
>>> new = op(w)
>>> new.x
Quantity['length'](Array(2., dtype=float32), unit='kpc')
```

```pycon
>>> new.t.uconvert("Gyr")
Quantity['time'](Array(0.99999994, dtype=float32), unit='Gyr')
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.translation.GalileanTranslationOperator.__call__
documentation:
Apply the translation to the coordinates.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

Explicitly construct the translation operator:

```pycon
>>> qshift = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 1, 1], "kpc")
>>> tshift = u.Quantity(1, "Gyr")
>>> shift = cx.FourVector(tshift, qshift)
>>> op = cxo.GalileanTranslationOperator(shift)
```

Construct a vector to translate

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "kpc")
>>> t = u.Quantity(1, "Gyr")
>>> newq, newt = op(q, t)
```

```pycon
>>> newq.x
Quantity['length'](Array(2., dtype=float32), unit='kpc')
```

```pycon
>>> newt
Quantity['time'](Array(2., dtype=float32), unit='Gyr')
```

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.operators.galilean.translation.GalileanTranslationOperator.translation
documentation:
The temporal + spatial translation.

The translation vector \[T, Q\].  This parameters uses
{meth}`vector.FourVector.from_` to enable a variety of more convenient
input types. See {class}`vector.FourVector` for details.

Operator for spatio-temporal translations.

The coordinate transform is given by:

$$
(t,\mathbf{x}) \mapsto (t+s, \mathbf{x} + \mathbf {a})
$$

where $a \in R^3$ and $s \in R$.  Therefore for a potential
$\Phi(t,\mathbf{x})$ in the translated frame the potential is given by
the subtraction of the translation.

# Parameters

translation

: The translation vector \[T, Q\].  This parameters uses
  {meth}`vector.FourVector.from_` to enable a variety of more
  convenient input types. See {class}`vector.FourVector` for details.

# Examples

We start with the required imports:

```pycon
>>> import quaxed.numpy as jnp
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> import coordinax.operators as cxo
```

We can then create a translation operator:

```pycon
>>> op = cxo.GalileanTranslationOperator.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0], "kpc")
>>> op
GalileanTranslationOperator(
  translation=FourVector(
    t=Quantity[PhysicalType('time')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("kpc s / km")),
    q=CartesianPos3D( ... ) )
)
```

Note that the translation is a {class}`vector.FourVector`, which was
constructed from a 1D array, using {meth}`vector.FourVector.from_`. We
can also construct it directly, which allows for other vector types.

```pycon
>>> qshift = cx.SphericalPos(r=u.Quantity(1.0, "kpc"),
...                          theta=u.Quantity(jnp.pi/2, "rad"),
...                          phi=u.Quantity(0, "rad"))
>>> shift = cx.FourVector(u.Quantity(1.0, "Gyr"), qshift)
>>> op = cxo.GalileanTranslationOperator(shift)
>>> op
GalileanTranslationOperator(
  translation=FourVector(
    t=Quantity[PhysicalType('time')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("Gyr")),
    q=SphericalPos( ... ) )
)
```

Translation operators can be applied to {class}`vector.FourVector`:

```pycon
>>> w = cx.FourVector.from_([0, 0, 0, 0], "kpc")
>>> op(w)
FourVector(
  t=Quantity[PhysicalType('time')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("kpc s / km")),
  q=CartesianPos3D( ... )
)
```

Also to {class}`vector.AbstractPos3D` and {class}`unxt.Quantity`:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([0, 0, 0], "kpc")
>>> t = u.Quantity(0, "Gyr")
>>> newq, newt = op(q, t)
>>> newq.x
Quantity['length'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='kpc')
>>> newt
Quantity['time'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='Gyr')
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.d1.transform.represent_as
documentation:
CartesianVel1D -> CartesianVel1D with no position.

Cartesian coordinates are an affine coordinate system and so the
transformation of an n-th order derivative vector in this system do not
require lower-order derivatives to be specified. See
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensors_in_curvilinear_coordinates> for more
information. This mixin provides a corresponding implementation of the
`coordinax.represent_as` method for Cartesian velocities.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> v = cx.CartesianVel1D.from_([1], "m/s")
>>> cx.represent_as(v, cx.CartesianVel1D) is v
True
```
CartesianVel1D

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.d1.transform.represent_as
documentation:
CartesianAcc1D -> CartesianAcc1D with no position.

Cartesian coordinates are an affine coordinate system and so the
transformation of an n-th order derivative vector in this system do not
require lower-order derivatives to be specified. See
<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensors_in_curvilinear_coordinates> for more
information. This mixin provides a corresponding implementation of the
`coordinax.represent_as` method for Cartesian vectors.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc1D.from_([1], "m/s2")
>>> cx.represent_as(a, cx.CartesianAcc1D) is a
True
```
CartesianAcc1D

modules.name: coordinax._src.vectors.space.compat
documentation: Space.

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.space.compat.fields
documentation:
Return the items from a Space.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from dataclassish import fields
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> x = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "km")
>>> v = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([4, 5, 6], "km/s")
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_([7, 8, 9], "km/s^2")
```

```pycon
>>> space = cx.Space(length=x, speed=v, acceleration=a)
```

```pycon
>>> fields(space)
(Field(name='length',type=<class 'coordinax...CartesianPos3D'>,...),
 Field(name='speed',type=<class 'coordinax...CartesianVel3D'>,...),
 Field(name='acceleration',type=<class 'coordinax...CartesianAcc3D'>,...))
```
NOTE: need to set the precedence because `Space` is both a `Mapping` and a
`dataclass`, which are both in the `field_items` dispatch table.

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.space.compat.field_keys


```pycon
>>> space = cx.Space(length=x, speed=v, acceleration=a)
```

```pycon
>>> field_keys(space)
dict_keys(['length', 'speed', 'acceleration'])
```
NOTE: need to set the precedence because `Space` is both a `Mapping` and a
`dataclass`, which are both in the `field_items` dispatch table.

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.space.compat.replace
documentation:
Replace the components of the vector.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from dataclassish import replace
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> x = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "km")
>>> v = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([4, 5, 6], "km/s")
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_([7, 8, 9], "km/s^2")
```

```pycon
>>> space = cx.Space(length=x, speed=v, acceleration=a)
>>> newspace = replace(space, length=cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([3, 2, 1], "km"))
>>> newspace["length"].x
Quantity['length'](Array(3., dtype=float32), unit='km')
```
NOTE: need to set the precedence because `Space` is both a `Mapping` and a
`dataclass`, which are both in the `replace` dispatch table.

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.d1.cartesian._add_aa
documentation:
Add two Cartesian accelerations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import quaxed.numpy as jnp
>>> from unxt import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> v = cx.CartesianAcc1D.from_([1], "km/s2")
>>> vec = jnp.add(v, v)
>>> vec
CartesianAcc1D(
    d2_x=Quantity[...](value=i32[], unit=Unit("km / s2"))
)
>>> vec.d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(2, dtype=int32), unit='km / s2')
```

```pycon
>>> (v + v).d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(2, dtype=int32), unit='km / s2')
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.d1.cartesian._mul_aq
documentation:
Scale an acceleration by a scalar.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import quaxed.numpy as jnp
>>> from unxt import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> v = cx.CartesianAcc1D(d2_x=Quantity(1, "m/s2"))
>>> vec = jnp.multiply(2, v)
>>> vec
CartesianAcc1D( d2_x=... )
```

```pycon
>>> vec.d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(2, dtype=int32, ...), unit='m / s2')
```

```pycon
>>> (2 * v).d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(2, dtype=int32, ...), unit='m / s2')
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.d1.cartesian._sub_a1_a1
documentation:
Subtract two 1-D cartesian accelerations.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from quaxed import lax
>>> from unxt import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = cx.CartesianAcc1D(d2_x=Quantity(1, "m/s2"))
>>> v2 = cx.CartesianAcc1D(d2_x=Quantity(2, "m/s2"))
>>> vec = lax.sub(v1, v2)
>>> vec
CartesianAcc1D( d2_x=... )
```

```pycon
>>> vec.d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(-1, dtype=int32, ...), unit='m / s2')
```

```pycon
>>> (v1 - v2).d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(-1, dtype=int32, ...), unit='m / s2')
```

classes.name: coordinax._src.vectors.d1.cartesian.CartesianPos1D

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.vectors.d1.cartesian.CartesianPos1D.x
documentation: X coordinate $x \in (-\infty,+\infty)$.

Cartesian vector representation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos1D.from_([2], "m")
>>> vec
CartesianPos1D(x=Quantity[PhysicalType('length')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")))
```

Vectors support the basic math operations:

```pycon
>>> (vec + vec).x
Quantity['length'](Array(4., dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> (vec - vec).x
Quantity['length'](Array(0., dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> (3 * vec).x
Quantity['length'](Array(6., dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

class_variables.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.attribute.VectorAttribute.name
documentation:
The name of the attribute on the Vector.

Cannot be set directly. Set by the container class when the attribute is
initialized.

Descriptor for attributes (non-coordinate fields) on a vector.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from coordinax import VectorAttribute, AbstractPos
```

```pycon
>>> class TestPos(AbstractPos):
...     x: int
...     attr: float = VectorAttribute(default=2.0)
```

vector-attributes are used to define fields on a vector that are not one of
the coordinates.

```pycon
>>> obj = TestPos(x=1)
>>> obj.components
('x',)
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.register_primitives._broadcast_in_dim_p
documentation:
Broadcast in a dimension.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import quaxed.numpy as jnp
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

Cartesian 1D position, velocity, and acceleration:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos1D.from_([1], "m")
>>> q.x
Quantity['length'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(q, (1, 1)).x
Quantity['length'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel1D.from_([1], "m/s")
>>> p.d_x
Quantity['speed'](Array(1, dtype=int32), unit='m / s')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(p, (1, 1)).d_x
Quantity['speed'](Array([1], dtype=int32), unit='m / s')
```

```pycon
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc1D.from_([1], "m/s2")
>>> a.d2_x
 Quantity['acceleration'](Array(1, dtype=int32), unit='m / s2')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(a, (1, 1)).d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array([1], dtype=int32), unit='m / s2')
```

Radial 1D position, velocity, and acceleration:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.RadialPos.from_([1], "m")
>>> q.r
Distance(Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(q, (1, 1)).r
Distance(Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> p = cx.RadialVel.from_([1], "m/s")
>>> p.d_r
Quantity['speed'](Array(1, dtype=int32), unit='m / s')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(p, (1, 1)).d_r
Quantity['speed'](Array([1], dtype=int32), unit='m / s')
```

```pycon
>>> a = cx.RadialAcc.from_([1], "m/s2")
>>> a.d2_r
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(1, dtype=int32), unit='m / s2')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(a, (1, 1)).d2_r
Quantity['acceleration'](Array([1], dtype=int32), unit='m / s2')
```

Cartesian 2D position, velocity, and acceleration:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_([1, 2], "m")
>>> q.x
Quantity['length'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(q, (1, 2)).x
Quantity['length'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel2D.from_([1, 2], "m/s")
>>> p.d_x
Quantity['speed'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='m / s')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(p, (1, 2)).d_x
Quantity['speed'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='m / s')
```

```pycon
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc2D.from_([1, 2], "m/s2")
>>> a.d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='m / s2')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(a, (1, 2)).d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='m / s2')
```

Cartesian 3D position, velocity, and acceleration:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m")
>>> q.x
Quantity['length'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(q, (1, 3)).x
Quantity['length'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

```pycon
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m/s")
>>> p.d_x
Quantity['speed'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='m / s')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(p, (1, 3)).d_x
Quantity['speed'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='m / s')
```

```pycon
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m/s2")
>>> a.d2_x
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(1., dtype=float32), unit='m / s2')
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.broadcast_to(a, (1, 3)).d2_x
 Quantity['acceleration'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='m / s2')
```

classes.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.flags.AttrFilter
documentation:
Flag to filter out `VectorAttribute` fields.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import dataclasses
>>> from dataclassish import field_items
>>> from coordinax import VectorAttribute, AttrFilter, AbstractPos
```

```pycon
>>> class TestPos(AbstractPos):
...    x: int
...    attr: float = VectorAttribute(default=2.0)
```

```pycon
>>> obj = TestPos(x=1)
```

```pycon
>>> [(f.name, getattr(obj, f.name)) for f in dataclasses.fields(obj)]
[('x', 1), ('attr', 2.0)]
```

```pycon
>>> field_items(obj)
(('x', 1), ('attr', 2.0))
```

```pycon
>>> field_items(AttrFilter, obj)
(('x', 1),)
```

classes.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.mixins.AvalMixin

methods.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.mixins.AvalMixin.aval
documentation:
Return the vector as a JAX array.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from unxt import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

1 dimensional vectors:

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos1D.from_([1], "m")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.RadialPos.from_([1], "m")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianVel1D.from_([1], "m/s")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1], dtype=int32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.RadialVel.from_([1], "m/s")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1], dtype=int32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianAcc1D.from_([1], "m/s2")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1], dtype=int32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.RadialAcc.from_([1], "m/s2")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1], dtype=int32)
```

2 dimensional vectors:

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_([1, 2], "m")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1. 2.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.PolarPos(r=Quantity(1, "m"), phi=Quantity(0, "rad"))
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1. 0.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianVel2D.from_([1, 2], "m/s")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1. 2.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.PolarVel(d_r=Quantity(1, "m/s"), d_phi=Quantity(0, "rad/s"))
>>> try: vec.aval()
... except NotImplementedError as e: print("nope")
nope
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianAcc2D.from_([1,2], "m/s2")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1. 2.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.PolarAcc(d2_r=Quantity(1, "m/s2"), d2_phi=Quantity(0, "rad/s2"))
>>> try: vec.aval()
... except NotImplementedError as e: print("nope")
nope
```

3 dimensional vectors:

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1. 2. 3.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]], "m")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([[1. 2. 3.]
               [4. 5. 6.]], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.SphericalPos(r=Quantity(1, "m"), phi=Quantity(0, "rad"), theta=Quantity(0, "rad"))
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([0. 0. 1.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1,2,3], "m/s")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1. 2. 3.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.SphericalVel(d_r=Quantity(1, "m/s"), d_phi=Quantity(0, "rad/s"), d_theta=Quantity(0, "rad/s"))
>>> try: vec.aval()
... except NotImplementedError as e: print("nope")
nope
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_([1,2,3], "m/s2")
>>> vec.aval()
ConcreteArray([1. 2. 3.], dtype=float32)
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.SphericalAcc(d2_r=Quantity(1, "m/s2"), d2_phi=Quantity(0, "rad/s2"), d2_theta=Quantity(0, "rad/s2"))
>>> try: vec.aval()
... except NotImplementedError as e: print("nope")
nope
```

Mixin class to add an `aval` method.

See \[quax doc\](<https://docs.kidger.site/quax/examples/custom_rules/>) for
more details.

classes.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.mixins.IPythonReprMixin

methods.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.mixins.IPythonReprMixin._repr_mimebundle_
documentation:
Return a MIME bundle representation of the Quantity.

# Parameters

include, exclude

: The set of keys to include / exclude in the MIME bundle. If not
  provided, all supported formats are included. 'include' has
  precedence over 'exclude'.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_([1, 2], "m")
>>> vec._repr_mimebundle_()
{'text/plain': 'CartesianPos2D(\n  x=Quantity[PhysicalType(\'length\')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),\n  y=Quantity[PhysicalType(\'length\')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))\n)',
 'text/latex': '$\\left( \\begin{matrix}\\mathrm{ x } \\\\ \\mathrm{ y }\\end{matrix} \\right)=\\left( \\begin{matrix}1. \\; \\mathrm{m} \\\\ 2. \\; \\mathrm{m}\\end{matrix} \\right)$'}
```

```pycon
>>> vec._repr_mimebundle_(include=["text/plain"])
{'text/plain': 'CartesianPos2D(\n  x=Quantity[PhysicalType(\'length\')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),\n  y=Quantity[PhysicalType(\'length\')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))\n)'}
```

```pycon
>>> vec._repr_mimebundle_(exclude=["text/latex"])
{'text/plain': 'CartesianPos2D(\n  x=Quantity[PhysicalType(\'length\')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),\n  y=Quantity[PhysicalType(\'length\')](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))\n)'}
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.mixins.IPythonReprMixin._repr_latex_

methods.name: coordinax._src.vectors.space.core.Space.__getitem__
documentation:
Get the vector by key.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> w = cx.Space(length=cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([[[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]], "m"),
...              speed=cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([[[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]], "m/s"))
```

By number:

```pycon
>>> w[0]
Space({
    'length': CartesianPos3D(
        x=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
        y=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
        z=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")) ),
    'speed': CartesianVel3D(
        d_x=Quantity[...]( value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_y=Quantity[...]( value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_z=Quantity[...]( value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m / s") ) )
})
```

By slice:

```pycon
>>> w[1:]
Space({
    'length': CartesianPos3D(
        x=Quantity[...](value=f32[0,2], unit=Unit("m")),
        y=Quantity[...](value=f32[0,2], unit=Unit("m")),
        z=Quantity[...](value=f32[0,2], unit=Unit("m")) ),
    'speed': CartesianVel3D(
        d_x=Quantity[...]( value=f32[0,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_y=Quantity[...]( value=f32[0,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_z=Quantity[...]( value=f32[0,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ) )
})
```

By Ellipsis:

```pycon
>>> w[...]
Space({
    'length': CartesianPos3D(
        x=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m")),
        y=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m")),
        z=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m")) ),
    'speed': CartesianVel3D(
        d_x=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_y=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_z=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ) )
})
```

By tuple\[int, ...\]:

```pycon
>>> w[(0, 1)]
Space({
    'length': CartesianPos3D(
        x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
        y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
        z=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")) ),
    'speed': CartesianVel3D(
        d_x=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_y=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_z=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("m / s") ) )
})
```

This also supports numpy index arrays. But this example section
highlights core python indexing.
Mapping API

```pycon
    z=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m"))
```

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
```

```pycon
    'length': CartesianPos3D(
        x=Quantity[...](value=f32[2,1], unit=Unit("m")),

        z=Quantity[...](value=f32[2,1], unit=Unit("m"))
    ), 'speed': CartesianVel3D(
        d_x=Quantity[...]( value=f32[2,1], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_y=Quantity[...]( value=f32[2,1], unit=Unit("m / s") ),

    ) })
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.vectors.space.core.Space.ndim
documentation:

A collection of vectors that acts like the primary vector.

# Parameters

args

: See input to `dict` for the input data.

primary_key

: The key for the primary vector of the space.
  Default is "length" for position vectors.

kwargs

: See input to `dict` for the input data.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> x = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "km")
>>> v = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([4, 5, 6], "km/s")
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_([7, 8, 9], "km/s^2")
```

```pycon
>>> space = cx.Space(length=x, speed=v, acceleration=a)
>>> space
Space({
    'length': CartesianPos3D( ... ),
    'speed': CartesianVel3D( ... ),
    'acceleration': CartesianAcc3D( ... )
})
```

```pycon
>>> space["length"]
CartesianPos3D( ... )
```

```pycon
>>> space.represent_as(cx.SphericalPos)
Space({
    'length': SphericalPos( ... ),
    'speed': SphericalVel( ... ),
    'acceleration': SphericalAcc( ... )
})
```

```pycon
>>> cx.represent_as(space, cx.SphericalPos)
Space({
    'length': SphericalPos( ... ),
    'speed': SphericalVel( ... ),
    'acceleration': SphericalAcc( ... )
})
```

eqx.Module differ.

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.base_pos._mul_v_pos
documentation:
Scale a position by a scalar.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from unxt import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> import quaxed.numpy as jnp
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m")
>>> jnp.multiply(2, vec)
CartesianPos3D(
  x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
  y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
  z=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

Most of the position classes have specific dispatches for this operation.
So let's define a new class and try it out:

```pycon
>>> from typing import ClassVar
>>> class MyCartesian(cx.AbstractPos):
...     x: Quantity
...     y: Quantity
...     z: Quantity
...
>>> MyCartesian._cartesian_cls = MyCartesian  # hack
```

Add conversion to Quantity:

```pycon
>>> from plum import conversion_method
>>> @conversion_method(MyCartesian, Quantity)
... def _to_quantity(x: MyCartesian, /) -> Quantity:
...     return jnp.stack((x.x, x.y, x.z), axis=-1)
```

Add representation transformation

```pycon
>>> from plum import dispatch
>>> @dispatch
... def represent_as(current: MyCartesian, target: type[MyCartesian], /) -> MyCartesian:
...     return current
```

```pycon
>>> vec = MyCartesian(x=Quantity([1], "m"),
...                   y=Quantity([2], "m"),
...                   z=Quantity([3], "m"))
```

First hit the non-scalar error:

```pycon
>>> try: jnp.multiply(jnp.asarray([[1, 1, 1]]), vec)
... except Exception as e: print(e)
must be a scalar, not <class 'jaxlib.xla_extension.ArrayImpl'>
```

Then hit the Cartesian-specific dispatch error:

```pycon
>>> try: jnp.multiply(2, vec)
... except Exception as e: print(e)
must register a Cartesian-specific dispatch
```

Now a real example. For this we need to define the Cartesian-specific
dispatches:

```pycon
>>> MyCartesian._cartesian_cls = cx.CartesianPos3D
>>> @dispatch
... def represent_as(current: MyCartesian, target: type[cx.CartesianPos3D], /) -> cx.CartesianPos3D:
...     return cx.CartesianPos3D(x=current.x, y=current.y, z=current.z)
>>> @dispatch
... def represent_as(current: cx.CartesianPos3D, target: type[MyCartesian], /) -> MyCartesian:
...     return MyCartesian(x=current.x, y=current.y, z=current.z)
```

```pycon
>>> jnp.multiply(2, vec)
MyCartesian(
  x=Quantity[...](value=f32[1], unit=Unit("m")),
  y=Quantity[...](value=f32[1], unit=Unit("m")),
  z=Quantity[...](value=f32[1], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.base_pos.AbstractPos.__eq__
documentation:
Element-wise equality of two positions.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import quaxed.numpy as jnp
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

Showing the broadcasting, then element-wise comparison of two vectors:

```pycon
>>> vec1 = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([[1, 2, 3], [1, 2, 4]], "m")
>>> vec2 = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m")
>>> jnp.equal(vec1, vec2)
Array([ True, False], dtype=bool)
```

Showing the change of representation:

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m")
>>> vec1 = vec.represent_as(cx.SphericalPos)
>>> vec2 = vec.represent_as(cx.MathSphericalPos)
>>> jnp.equal(vec1, vec2)
Array(True, dtype=bool)
```

Quick run-through of each dimensionality:

```pycon
>>> vec1 = cx.CartesianPos1D.from_([1], "m")
>>> vec2 = cx.RadialPos.from_([1], "m")
>>> jnp.equal(vec1, vec2)
Array(True, dtype=bool)
```

```pycon
>>> vec1 = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_([2, 0], "m")
>>> vec2 = cx.PolarPos(r=Quantity(2, "m"), phi=Quantity(0, "rad"))
>>> jnp.equal(vec1, vec2)
Array(True, dtype=bool)
```

modules.name: coordinax._src.vectors.transform.differentials
documentation: Transformations between representations.

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.transform.differentials.represent_as
documentation:
AbstractVel -> Cartesian -> AbstractVel.

This is the base case for the transformation of vector differentials.

# Parameters

current

: The vector differential to transform.

target

: The target type of the vector differential.

position

: The position vector used to transform the differential.

kwargs

: Additional keyword arguments.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

Let's start in 1D:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos1D(x=Quantity(1.0, "km"))
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel1D(d_x=Quantity(1.0, "km/s"))
>>> cx.represent_as(p, cx.RadialVel, q)
RadialVel( d_r=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("km / s") ) )
```

Now in 2D:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_([1.0, 2.0], "km")
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel2D.from_([1.0, 2.0], "km/s")
>>> cx.represent_as(p, cx.PolarVel, q)
PolarVel(
  d_r=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("km / s") ),
  d_phi=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s") )
)
```

And in 3D:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km")
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km/s")
>>> cx.represent_as(p, cx.SphericalVel, q)
SphericalVel(
  d_r=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("km / s") ),
  d_theta=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s") ),
  d_phi=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s") )
)
```

If given a position as a Quantity, it will be converted to the appropriate
Cartesian vector:

```pycon
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km/s")
>>> cx.represent_as(p, cx.SphericalVel, Quantity([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km"))
SphericalVel(
  d_r=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("km / s") ),
  d_theta=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s") ),
  d_phi=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s") )
)
```

`coordinax.AbstractPos` -> `unxt.AbstractQuantity`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> from unxt.quantity import AbstractQuantity, Quantity
```

```pycon
>>> pos = cx.CartesianPos1D.from_([1.0], "km")
>>> convert(pos, AbstractQuantity)
Quantity['length'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='km')
```

```pycon
>>> pos = cx.RadialPos.from_([1.0], "km")
>>> convert(pos, AbstractQuantity)
Quantity['length'](Array([1.], dtype=float32), unit='km')
```

```pycon
>>> pos = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_([1.0, 2.0], "km")
>>> convert(pos, AbstractQuantity)
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 2.], dtype=float32), unit='km')
```

```pycon
>>> pos = cx.PolarPos(Quantity(1.0, "km"), Quantity(0, "deg"))
>>> convert(pos, AbstractQuantity)
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 0.], dtype=float32), unit='km')
```

```pycon
>>> pos = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km")
>>> convert(pos, AbstractQuantity)
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 2., 3.], dtype=float32), unit='km')
```

```pycon
>>> pos = cx.SphericalPos(Quantity(1.0, "km"), Quantity(0, "deg"), Quantity(0, "deg"))
>>> convert(pos, AbstractQuantity)
Quantity['length'](Array([0., 0., 1.], dtype=float32), unit='km')
```

```pycon
>>> pos = cx.CylindricalPos(Quantity(1.0, "km"), Quantity(0, "deg"), Quantity(0, "km"))
>>> convert(pos, AbstractQuantity)
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 0., 0.], dtype=float32), unit='km')
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.compat.uconvert
documentation:
Convert the vector to the given units.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> usys = u.unitsystem("m", "s", "kg", "rad")
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "km")
>>> u.uconvert(usys, vec)
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```
Compatibility with unxt

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.compat.uconvert
documentation:
Convert the vector to the given units.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

We can convert a vector to the given units:

```pycon
>>> cart = cx.CartesianPos2D(x=Quantity(1, "m"), y=Quantity(2, "km"))
>>> cart.uconvert({"length": "km"})
CartesianPos2D(
    x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km")),
    y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km"))
)
```

This also works for vectors with different units:

```pycon
>>> sph = cx.SphericalPos(r=Quantity(1, "m"), theta=Quantity(45, "deg"),
...                       phi=Quantity(3, "rad"))
>>> sph.uconvert({"length": "km", "angle": "deg"})
SphericalPos(
    r=Distance(value=f32[], unit=Unit("km")),
    theta=Angle(value=f32[], unit=Unit("deg")),
    phi=Angle(value=f32[], unit=Unit("deg")) )
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.compat.uconvert
documentation:
Convert the vector to a self-consistent set of units.

# Parameters

flag

: The vector is converted to consistent units by looking for the first
  quantity with each physical type and converting all components to
  the units of that quantity.

vector

: The vector to convert.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import unxt as u
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

We can convert a vector to the given units:

```pycon
>>> cart = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_([1, 2], "m")
```

If all you want is to convert to consistent units, you can use
`"consistent"`:

```pycon
>>> cart.uconvert(cx.ToUnitsOptions.consistent)
CartesianPos2D(
    x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

```pycon
>>> sph = cart.represent_as(cx.SphericalPos)
>>> sph.uconvert(cx.ToUnitsOptions.consistent)
SphericalPos(
    r=Distance(value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    theta=Angle(value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad")),
    phi=Angle(value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad"))
)
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.compat.fields
documentation:
Return the fields of an object, filtering out VectorAttribute\`s.

# Examples

An example vector class with a `VectorAttribute` field is `FourVector`.

```pycon
>>> from dataclassish import fields
>>> import unxt as u
>>> from coordinax import FourVector, AttrFilter
```

```pycon
>>> w = cx.FourVector(t=u.Quantity(1, "s"), q=u.Quantity([1, 2, 3], "m"))
>>> [f.name for f in fields(AttrFilter, w)]
['t', 'q']
```

modules.name: coordinax._src.vectors.transform.accelerations
documentation: Transformations between representations.

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.transform.accelerations.represent_as
documentation:
AbstractAcc -> Cartesian -> AbstractAcc.

This is the base case for the transformation of accelerations.

# Parameters

current

: The vector acceleration to transform.

target

: The target type of the vector acceleration.

velocity

: The velocity vector used to transform the acceleration.

position

: The position vector used to transform the acceleration.

kwargs

: Additional keyword arguments.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
>>> from unxt import Quantity
```

Let's start in 1D:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos1D(x=Quantity(1.0, "km"))
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel1D(d_x=Quantity(1.0, "km/s"))
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc1D(d2_x=Quantity(1.0, "km/s2"))
>>> cx.represent_as(a, cx.RadialAcc, p, q)
RadialAcc( d2_r=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km / s2")) )
```

Now in 2D:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos2D.from_([1.0, 2.0], "km")
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel2D.from_([1.0, 2.0], "km/s")
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc2D.from_([1.0, 2.0], "km/s2")
>>> cx.represent_as(a, cx.PolarAcc, p, q)
PolarAcc(
  d2_r=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km / s2")),
  d2_phi=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s2") )
)
```

And in 3D:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km")
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km/s")
>>> a = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km/s2")
>>> cx.represent_as(a, cx.SphericalAcc, p, q)
SphericalAcc(
  d2_r=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km / s2")),
  d2_theta=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s2") ),
  d2_phi=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s2") )
)
```

If given a position as a Quantity, it will be converted to the appropriate
Cartesian vector:

```pycon
>>> cx.represent_as(a, cx.SphericalAcc,
...                 Quantity([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km/s"),
...                 Quantity([1.0, 2.0, 3.0], "km"))
SphericalAcc(
  d2_r=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km / s2")),
  d2_theta=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s2") ),
  d2_phi=Quantity[...]( value=f32[], unit=Unit("rad / s2") )
)
```
TODO: implement for cross-representations

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.transform.dn.represent_as
documentation:
Space -> PoincarePolarVector.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> w = cx.Space(
...     length=cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([[[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]], "m"),
...     speed=cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([[[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]], "m/s")
... )
```

```pycon
>>> cx.represent_as(w, cx.PoincarePolarVector)
PoincarePolarVector(
    rho=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m")),
    pp_phi=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m rad(1/2) / s(1/2)") ),
    z=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m")),
    d_rho=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
    d_pp_phi=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m rad(1/2) / s(1/2)") ),
    d_z=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m / s") )
)
```

```pycon
>>> cx.represent_as(w, cx.Space)
Space({
    'length': CartesianPos3D(
        x=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m")),
        y=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m")),
        z=Quantity[...](value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m"))
    ),
    'speed': CartesianVel3D(
        d_x=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_y=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m / s") ),
        d_z=Quantity[...]( value=f32[1,2], unit=Unit("m / s") )
    )
})
```

```pycon
>>> from unxt import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> x = cx.CylindricalPos(rho=Quantity(1.0, "km"), phi=Quantity(10.0, "deg"),
...                          z=Quantity(14, "km"))
```

```pycon
>>> with warnings.catch_warnings():
...     warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
```
```pycon
...     x2 = cx.represent_as(x, cx.PolarPos)
>>> x2
PolarPos( r=Distance(value=f32[], unit=Unit("km")),
          phi=Angle(value=f32[], unit=Unit("deg")) )
```

functions.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.base.from_
documentation:
Construct a vector from another vector.

# Parameters

cls

: The vector class.

obj

: The vector to construct from.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

Positions:

```pycon
>>> q = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "km")
```

```pycon
>>> cart = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(q)
>>> cart
CartesianPos3D(
  x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km")),
  y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km")),
  z=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("km"))
)
```

```pycon
>>> cx.AbstractPos3D.from_(cart) is cart
True
```

```pycon
>>> sph = cart.represent_as(cx.SphericalPos)
>>> cx.AbstractPos3D.from_(sph) is sph
True
```

```pycon
>>> cyl = cart.represent_as(cx.CylindricalPos)
>>> cx.AbstractPos3D.from_(cyl) is cyl
True
```

Velocities:

```pycon
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "km/s")
```

```pycon
>>> cart = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_(p)
>>> cx.AbstractVel3D.from_(cart) is cart
True
```

```pycon
>>> sph = cart.represent_as(cx.SphericalVel, q)
>>> cx.AbstractVel3D.from_(sph) is sph
True
```

```pycon
>>> cyl = cart.represent_as(cx.CylindricalVel, q)
>>> cx.AbstractVel3D.from_(cyl) is cyl
True
```

Accelerations:

```pycon
>>> p = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1, 1, 1], "km/s")
```

```pycon
>>> cart = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "km/s2")
>>> cx.AbstractAcc3D.from_(cart) is cart
True
```

```pycon
>>> sph = cart.represent_as(cx.SphericalAcc, p, q)
>>> cx.AbstractAcc3D.from_(sph) is sph
True
```

```pycon
>>> cyl = cart.represent_as(cx.CylindricalAcc, p, q)
>>> cx.AbstractAcc3D.from_(cyl) is cyl
True
```

Construct a vector from a mapping.

# Parameters

obj

: The mapping of components.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import jax.numpy as jnp

```

```pycon

...       "z": u.Quantity(3, "m")}

>>> vec
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

```pycon
>>> xs = {"x": u.Quantity([1, 2], "m"), "y": u.Quantity([3, 4], "m"),
...       "z": u.Quantity([5, 6], "m")}
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(xs)
>>> vec
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.base.AbstractVector.from_
documentation:
Construct a vector from an array and unit.

The array is expected to have the components as the last dimension.

# Parameters

obj

: The array of components.

unit

: The unit of the quantity

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import jax.numpy as jnp
>>> from unxt import Quantity
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

```pycon
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "meter")
>>> vec
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[...](value=f32[], unit=Unit("m"))
)
```

```pycon
>>> xs = jnp.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]])
>>> vec = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_(xs, "meter")
>>> vec
CartesianPos3D(
    x=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
    y=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m")),
    z=Quantity[...](value=f32[2], unit=Unit("m"))
)
>>> vec.x
Quantity['length'](Array([1., 4.], dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

methods.name: coordinax._src.vectors.base.base.AbstractVector.represent_as
documentation:
Represent the vector as another type.

This just forwards to `coordinax.represent_as`.

# Parameters

target

: The type to represent the vector as.

args, kwargs

: Extra arguments. These are passed to `coordinax.represent_as` and
  might be used, depending on the dispatched method. E.g. for
  transforming an acceleration, generally the first argument is the
  velocity (`coordinax.AbstractVel`) followed by the position
  (`coordinax.AbstractPos`) at which the acceleration is defined. In
  general this is a required argument, though it is not for
  Cartesian-to-Cartesian transforms -- see
  <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tensors_in_curvilinear_coordinates> for
  more information.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> import coordinax as cx
```

Transforming a Position:

```pycon
>>> q_cart = cx.CartesianPos3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m")
>>> q_sph = q_cart.represent_as(cx.SphericalPos)
>>> q_sph
SphericalPos( ... )
>>> q_sph.r
Distance(Array(3.7416575, dtype=float32), unit='m')
```

Transforming a Velocity:

```pycon
>>> v_cart = cx.CartesianVel3D.from_([1, 2, 3], "m/s")
>>> v_sph = v_cart.represent_as(cx.SphericalVel, q_cart)
>>> v_sph
SphericalVel( ... )
```

Transforming an Acceleration:

```pycon
>>> a_cart = cx.CartesianAcc3D.from_([7, 8, 9], "m/s2")
>>> a_sph = a_cart.represent_as(cx.SphericalAcc, v_cart, q_cart)
>>> a_sph
SphericalAcc( ... )
>>> a_sph.d2_r
Quantity['acceleration'](Array(13.363062, dtype=float32), unit='m / s2')
```
